entry.title: Azure Batch
entry.description: Azure Batch enables large-scale parallel and high-performance computing jobs in the cloud with automatic scaling and job scheduling.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 750 hours/month of low-priority batch compute nodes.
caveats: Storage, networking, and premium VM types billed separately
Low-priority nodes may be preempted
billingRiskNotes: Exceeding free hours or using premium resources incurs charges

quickstartSteps: Sign in to the Azure Portal and create a Batch account.
Set up a Batch pool with the desired VM size and configuration.
Upload your application and input data to Azure Storage.
Create and submit a job with tasks to the Batch pool.
Monitor job progress and download results upon completion.
entry.whenToUse: Use Azure Batch when you need to run parallel or high-volume compute jobs without managing infrastructure.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require guaranteed compute availability or need persistent, stateful workloads.
entry.body: Azure Batch provides scalable, managed batch computing for parallel workloads, offering a free tier for low-priority compute and easy job orchestration.

entry.title: Azure Container Apps
entry.description: Azure Container Apps enables you to run microservices and containerized applications without managing infrastructure.

freeTierDetails.summary: Includes 180,000 vCPU-seconds, 360,000 GiB-seconds, and 2 million requests per month free.
caveats: Free tier applies to each subscription.
Overages billed at standard rates.
billingRiskNotes: Usage beyond free tier is automatically billed.
No hard cap; monitor usage to avoid unexpected charges.

quickstartSteps: Sign in to the Azure Portal.
Create a new Container Apps environment.
Deploy your container image from a registry.
Configure scaling and ingress settings.
Test your deployed application endpoint.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you want to deploy containerized applications or microservices without managing Kubernetes or underlying infrastructure.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you need full Kubernetes control, persistent storage, or require hard usage caps to prevent overages.
entry.body: Azure Container Apps provides a serverless platform for running containers and microservices, offering automatic scaling and integrated ingress with a generous free tier.

entry.title: Azure Container Registry
entry.description: Azure Container Registry provides private Docker container image storage and management for Azure deployments.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier (Basic SKU) includes 1 GiB storage and 10 webhooks per registry.
caveats: Limited performance and features on Basic tier
No geo-replication or content trust
billingRiskNotes: Exceeding storage or features requires upgrade to paid SKU

quickstartSteps: Sign in to Azure Portal and create a Container Registry resource.
Select the Basic SKU for the free tier.
Configure access permissions for your team or service principals.
Push your Docker images to the registry using Azure CLI or Docker CLI.
Pull images from the registry in your deployments.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need private container image storage tightly integrated with Azure services and simple access control.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require advanced features like geo-replication, high throughput, or multi-cloud registry integration.
entry.body: Azure Container Registry offers a managed, private Docker registry service for storing and managing container images within Azure, suitable for basic workloads and CI/CD integration.

entry.title: Azure Functions
entry.description: Azure Functions is a serverless compute service for running event-driven code without managing infrastructure.

freeTierDetails.summary: 1 million free requests and 400,000 GB-s of resource consumption per month.
caveats: Premium and Dedicated plans not included
Networking features may incur additional costs
billingRiskNotes: Charges apply if usage exceeds free tier limits

quickstartSteps: Sign in to the Azure Portal.
Create a new Function App resource.
Choose your runtime stack and region.
Add a function using the portal or VS Code extension.
Configure a trigger (HTTP, timer, etc.) and deploy your code.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need to run code in response to events or triggers without managing servers.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require long-running processes, low-latency guarantees, or full control over the underlying infrastructure.
entry.body: Azure Functions lets you build and deploy event-driven applications with minimal configuration, offering a generous free tier for lightweight and scalable workloads.

entry.title: Azure Kubernetes Service
entry.description: Managed Kubernetes clusters for deploying, scaling, and managing containerized applications on Azure.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes no charge for cluster management and one free node pool; you pay for VM nodes and other resources.
limits: One free node pool per cluster
Pay for VM nodes, storage, and networking
caveats: Compute, storage, and network resources incur standard Azure charges
Free tier does not cover node VM costs
billingRiskNotes: You may incur charges if you provision VM nodes or additional resources

quickstartSteps: Sign in to the Azure Portal and create a new Kubernetes Service resource.
Configure cluster basics, including region and Kubernetes version.
Set up the default node pool and select VM size (note: VMs are billed separately).
Review and create the cluster.
Install and configure kubectl to connect to your AKS cluster.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need a managed Kubernetes environment for deploying and scaling containerized workloads without managing control plane infrastructure.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require a fully free compute environment or want to run containers without any VM or resource charges.
entry.body: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) provides a managed Kubernetes platform for deploying, scaling, and operating containerized applications with integrated Azure services.

entry.title: Azure VM Image Builder
entry.description: Azure VM Image Builder automates creation, customization, and management of VM images at scale in Azure.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 20 custom image builds per month.
caveats: Standard VM and storage costs apply for resources used during image creation.
billingRiskNotes: Additional builds incur standard charges.
Resource usage during builds may generate extra costs.

useCases: Integrating image builds into CI/CD pipelines
quickstartSteps: Sign in to the Azure portal and ensure you have a subscription.
Register the Image Builder resource provider in your subscription.
Create an Azure Image Builder template specifying source image, customizations, and destination.
Deploy the template to start the image build process.
Retrieve and use the custom image for VM deployments.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need to automate and standardize VM image creation for consistent deployments across Azure environments.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require image building for non-Azure clouds or need advanced configuration management outside of supported customizations.
avoidIf: You have highly specialized OS customization needs
entry.body: Azure VM Image Builder streamlines the process of creating and managing custom VM images, enabling automation and consistency for Azure-based deployments.

entry.title: Cloud Functions
entry.description: Google Cloud Functions provides event-driven, serverless compute for running code in response to HTTP requests or cloud events.

freeTierDetails.summary: Generous always-free tier with monthly limits on invocations, compute time, and outbound data.
caveats: Limits apply per billing account, not per project.
Outbound networking beyond free tier is billed.
Cold starts may affect latency.
billingRiskNotes: Overages are billed automatically if limits are exceeded.
Monitor usage to avoid unexpected charges.

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a Google Cloud account and enable billing.
Enable the Cloud Functions API in your project.
Install and initialize the Google Cloud SDK.
Write your function code and define a trigger (HTTP or event).
Deploy your function using the gcloud CLI.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need to run code in response to events or HTTP requests without managing servers.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid for long-running processes, high-throughput workloads, or when you need fine-grained control over runtime environments.
entry.body: Google Cloud Functions offers a scalable, event-driven compute platform ideal for lightweight APIs, automation, and integrating cloud services without server management.

entry.title: Cloud Run
entry.description: Cloud Run is a fully managed serverless platform for running containerized applications on Google Cloud.

freeTierDetails.summary: Cloud Run offers a generous always-free tier for containerized HTTP workloads.
caveats: Only applies to one region per billing account
Overages billed per usage
Free tier does not cover custom domains or all regions
billingRiskNotes: Usage beyond free tier is billed automatically
Monitor usage to avoid unexpected charges

quickstartSteps: Install and configure the Google Cloud CLI.
Build your application into a container image.
Push the container image to Google Container Registry or Artifact Registry.
Deploy the container to Cloud Run using the gcloud CLI.
Access your service via the generated HTTPS endpoint.
entry.whenToUse: Use Cloud Run when you need to deploy containerized HTTP services without managing infrastructure or scaling.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid Cloud Run for stateful workloads, long-running background tasks, or when you require persistent connections.
entry.body: Cloud Run lets you deploy and scale containerized applications easily, with a generous free tier suitable for prototyping and small production workloads.

entry.title: Cloudflare Workers
entry.description: Cloudflare Workers lets you deploy serverless code globally at the edge for fast, scalable web applications and APIs.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 100,000 requests per day with no credit card required.
caveats: KV storage and Durable Objects have separate limits.
No custom domains on free tier.

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a Cloudflare account.
Install Wrangler CLI: npm install -g wrangler.
Run wrangler login to authenticate.
Create a new project: wrangler init my-worker.
Deploy with wrangler publish.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need fast, global serverless functions for APIs, static sites, or lightweight backend logic with minimal setup.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require long-running processes, large memory, or advanced networking features not supported by Workers.
entry.body: Cloudflare Workers enables developers to deploy serverless code at the edge, offering fast, scalable, and globally distributed compute for APIs and web applications.

entry.title: Cloudflare Workers KV
entry.description: Cloudflare Workers KV is a globally distributed key-value storage for serverless applications running on Cloudflare Workers.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 1 GB storage, 100,000 reads, and 1,000 writes per day.
caveats: Eventual consistency
No strong consistency guarantees
Performance varies by region

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a Cloudflare account and log in.
Create a new namespace in the Workers KV dashboard.
Install Wrangler CLI and configure your project.
Bind the KV namespace to your Worker script.
Deploy your Worker and interact with KV using the API.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need low-latency, globally distributed key-value storage for serverless or edge applications.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require strong consistency, high write throughput, or complex querying beyond simple key-value access.
entry.body: Cloudflare Workers KV provides simple, globally distributed key-value storage for serverless applications, ideal for edge caching, configuration, and session data with eventual consistency.

entry.title: Compute Engine e2-micro
entry.description: Google Cloud's e2-micro VM offers always-free, low-cost virtual machines for lightweight workloads and testing.

freeTierDetails.summary: Always-free e2-micro VM in select US regions with 30 GB HDD and 1 GB outbound traffic per month.
limits: 1 e2-micro VM per month (US regions only)
caveats: Only available in select US regions
Performance suitable for low-traffic or test workloads
billingRiskNotes: Charges apply if you exceed free limits or use premium resources

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a Google Cloud account and enable billing.
Navigate to Compute Engine and activate the API.
Create a new VM instance, selecting 'e2-micro' as the machine type and a supported US region.
Configure disk (up to 30 GB standard persistent disk) and firewall settings as needed.
Deploy and connect to your VM via SSH from the Cloud Console.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need a free, always-on VM for development, testing, or learning in a US region with minimal resource needs.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require high performance, global regions, or production-grade reliability for critical workloads.
entry.body: Google Cloud's Compute Engine e2-micro VM offers an always-free virtual machine for lightweight workloads, ideal for learning, prototyping, and personal projects in select US regions.

entry.title: EMQX Serverless
entry.description: EMQX Serverless provides fully managed, scalable MQTT messaging for IoT and real-time applications without server management.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 1 million messages and 100,000 minutes of connection time per month.
caveats: Overages incur charges
Limited to MQTT protocol
billingRiskNotes: Usage beyond free limits is billed automatically.

quickstartSteps: Sign up for an EMQX Cloud account.
Create a new Serverless deployment from the dashboard.
Configure your MQTT topic and connection settings.
Connect your device or app using the provided MQTT endpoint.
Monitor usage and messages in the EMQX dashboard.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need scalable, managed MQTT messaging for IoT or real-time apps without server maintenance.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require protocols other than MQTT or need strict hard caps to prevent overages.
entry.body: EMQX Serverless offers managed MQTT messaging with a generous free tier, ideal for IoT and real-time applications needing quick setup and scaling.

entry.title: Firebase Cloud Functions
entry.description: Firebase Cloud Functions lets you run backend code in response to events triggered by Firebase features and HTTPS requests.

freeTierDetails.summary: Generous always-free tier with monthly limits on invocations, compute time, and outbound networking.
caveats: Limits apply per billing account, not per project.
Outbound networking beyond 5GB is billed.
Some regions may have different quotas.
billingRiskNotes: Exceeding free tier limits results in automatic billing.
No hard cap; monitor usage to avoid unexpected charges.

useCases: Trigger backend logic on Firestore or Auth events
Build REST APIs for mobile/web apps
quickstartSteps: Install Firebase CLI and initialize your project with 'firebase init functions'.
Write your function code in 'functions/index.js' or 'functions/src/index.ts'.
Deploy your functions using 'firebase deploy --only functions'.
Test your deployed functions via Firebase console or HTTPS endpoint.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need to run backend code in response to Firebase events or HTTPS requests without managing servers.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require long-running processes, custom runtimes, or need strict hard caps to prevent billing overages.
entry.body: Firebase Cloud Functions provides a scalable, serverless environment for running backend code triggered by Firebase events or HTTPS requests, with a generous free tier suitable for most small-to-medium projects.

entry.title: Google Kubernetes Engine
entry.description: Managed Kubernetes service for deploying, managing, and scaling containerized applications on Google Cloud.

freeTierDetails.summary: One zonal cluster per month free, excluding node and network costs.
limits: Node VM and storage billed separately
caveats: Only control plane fee is free; compute and storage incur charges.
Regional clusters not included in free tier.
billingRiskNotes: Node and network usage can incur costs even if control plane is free.

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a Google Cloud account and enable billing.
Enable the Kubernetes Engine API in your project.
Install and configure the Google Cloud CLI (gcloud).
Create a zonal GKE cluster using the gcloud CLI or Cloud Console.
Deploy your containerized app using kubectl.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need a managed Kubernetes environment for containerized workloads with Google Cloud integration and automated cluster management.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require a fully free compute environment or need regional clusters without incurring costs.
entry.body: Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) offers a managed Kubernetes platform with automated operations, integrated security, and scalability for containerized workloads on Google Cloud. The free tier covers the control plane fee for one zonal cluster per month, but node, storage, and network costs apply. Ideal for teams deploying production-grade Kubernetes clusters with Google Cloud integration.

entry.title: Khan Academy
entry.description: Khan Academy offers free interactive courses and coding exercises for learning programming and computer science fundamentals.

freeTierDetails.summary: All courses and exercises are free with no payment required.

quickstartSteps: Go to the Khan Academy programming section.
Sign up for a free account or log in.
Browse available programming courses or topics.
Start an interactive lesson or coding exercise.
Track your progress as you complete activities.
entry.whenToUse: Use Khan Academy to learn programming fundamentals or practice coding interactively at your own pace, especially if you are a beginner.
entry.whenNotToUse: Do not use if you need advanced, specialized programming topics or require formal certification for professional purposes.
entry.body: Khan Academy provides free, interactive programming courses and exercises suitable for beginners, making it ideal for self-paced foundational learning without cost.

entry.title: Lambda
entry.description: AWS Lambda runs code in response to events without provisioning or managing servers.

freeTierDetails.summary: 1M free requests and 400,000 GB-seconds compute time per month.
caveats: Free tier applies per account, not per function.
Charges apply for additional usage and other AWS resources invoked.
billingRiskNotes: Overages can incur charges if limits are exceeded.
Linked AWS services may generate additional costs.

quickstartSteps: Sign up for an AWS account and log in to the AWS Management Console.
Navigate to AWS Lambda and click 'Create function'.
Choose 'Author from scratch', enter a function name, and select a runtime.
Write or upload your function code and configure the handler.
Set up a trigger (e.g., API Gateway, S3 event) and deploy the function.
Test the function using the AWS Console or configured trigger.
entry.whenToUse: Use Lambda for running code in response to events without managing servers, especially for variable workloads or microservices.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid Lambda for long-running processes, high-throughput workloads, or when you need predictable, sustained compute resources.
entry.body: AWS Lambda is a widely-used serverless compute service that lets you run code in response to events, with a generous free tier suitable for prototyping, automation, and scalable microservices. The free tier covers most small workloads, but be mindful of overages and costs from other AWS services triggered by Lambda functions.

entry.title: Oracle Always Free Compute
entry.description: Oracle Cloud offers always-free virtual machines with limited resources for development, testing, and lightweight production workloads.

freeTierDetails.summary: Always-free tier includes up to 4 ARM-based VMs or 2 AMD-based VMs with limited resources.
limits: 2 AMD-based VMs (1 OCPU, 1 GB RAM each)
caveats: Limited to specific shapes and regions
No GPU or high-performance options
Subject to Oracle's fair use policy

quickstartSteps: Sign up for an Oracle Cloud Free Tier account.
Verify your email and add a payment method (credit card required for verification).
Navigate to the Compute section in the Oracle Cloud Console.
Create a new VM instance using the Always Free eligible shapes.
Configure networking and storage as needed, then launch your VM.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need free, persistent VMs for development, testing, or lightweight workloads with predictable resource limits.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require high performance, GPU, large-scale deployments, or advanced networking features not available in the free tier.
entry.body: Oracle Always Free Compute provides a no-cost way to run small virtual machines suitable for development, learning, and lightweight workloads, with strict resource limits and no overage risk.

entry.title: Supabase Edge Functions
entry.description: Deploy TypeScript/JavaScript serverless functions at the edge, tightly integrated with Supabase projects.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 500,000 function invocations per month per project.
caveats: Limits apply per project, not per account.
Overages may incur charges.
billingRiskNotes: Exceeding free tier limits may result in automatic billing if a payment method is added.

quickstartSteps: Sign up and create a Supabase project.
Install the Supabase CLI and log in.
Run 'supabase functions new my-function' to scaffold a function.
Write your TypeScript/JavaScript handler in the generated file.
Deploy with 'supabase functions deploy my-function'.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need lightweight, serverless APIs or event handlers integrated with your Supabase backend.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require heavy compute, long-running processes, or need more than 60 seconds execution per function.
entry.body: Supabase Edge Functions let you deploy serverless TypeScript/JavaScript functions close to your users, ideal for APIs, webhooks, and custom backend logic within Supabase projects.

entry.title: Virtual Machines
entry.description: Azure Virtual Machines provide scalable, on-demand compute resources for running applications, development, and testing workloads in the cloud.

freeTierDetails.summary: 750 hours per month of B1S Windows or Linux VM for 12 months.
caveats: Standard HDD only
Limited to select regions
After 12 months, standard rates apply
billingRiskNotes: Exceeding hours or using premium resources incurs charges after free tier is exhausted.

quickstartSteps: Sign up for an Azure account and activate the free tier.
Navigate to the Azure Portal and select 'Create a resource'.
Choose 'Virtual Machine', select the B1S size, and configure OS and region.
Set up authentication and networking as required.
Review and create the VM, then connect via SSH or RDP.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need flexible, on-demand compute for development, testing, or lightweight production workloads with minimal upfront cost.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require persistent, high-performance workloads, specialized hardware, or want to avoid potential overage charges after the free tier.
entry.body: Azure Virtual Machines offer a flexible way to run applications and workloads in the cloud, ideal for learning, development, and small-scale deployments with a generous free tier for new users.
